turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has the opinion of the court this morning in Case 23-861, Feliciano v. The Department of Transportation.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Tens of thousands of federal civilian employees also serve the Nation as military reservists. When the military calls these reservists to active duty, it often pays them less than they earn in their civilian jobs. To address that gap, Congress has adopted a differential pay statute. That statute requires the government to make up the difference between a federal civilian employee's military and civilian pay under various circumstances, including when a reservist is called to active duty during a national emergency. This case poses a question about the meaning of that phrase during a national emergency. Does it promise differential pay to federal civilian employees who are called to active duty while a national emergency is ongoing as Mr. Feliciano argues, or does it also require a reservist seeking differential pay to prove some kind of substantive connection between his active duty service in a particular national emergency as the government contends. Several considerations persuade us that Mr. Feliciano's interpretation is a better one. I will mention just a few of them here. First, start with the statutory term on which this dispute really hinges, the word "during". Everyone agrees it is a matter of ordinary meaning that word requires a temporal link between the reservist service and an ongoing emergency. So reservist is entitled differential pay only if he serves while a national emergency is ongoing, but what in the word "during" indicates that when deferential pay, a reservist must also show some substantive connection between his particular service in a particular emergency is a lot to demand of a single word, especially when this court and the government itself have in the past said that the word "during" denotes a temporal connection only and generally does not imply a substitute one. Second, when insisting on both the temporal and substantive connection in other statutory settings, Congress has usually used more than the word "during" to make the point. Up and down the Federal Criminal Code, for example, statutes speak of actions taken during and in relation to certain kinds of criminal conduct. Those additional words signal that the conduct in question must bear both the temporal and substantive connection some specified condition, but we have nothing like that here, and the fact that Congress chose a different approach in this setting is to our minds a clue that it meant for this law to work differently. Third, another provision related to certain reservists discusses when they are called to active duty in time of national emergency. The government contends that this statutory language speaks only temporarily. As a result, the government says it guarantees differential pay for reservist called to active duty while an emergency is ongoing even if their service does not bear a substantive connection to that emergency. But if that's true, we struggle to see why the provision before us should be read differently. Finally, suppose some substance connection really were required, what should it be? In proceedings below, the Federal Circuit suggested that a reservist seeking differential pay must show that he served directly in a military operation responding to a particular emergency. Before us, the government advocates for more forgiving standard, one that would require a reservist to show only that his service was in support of a military operation. The fact that the statutory language before us applies no principled way to choose between these two options, strikes us as another sign yet that the law does not require anything of the sort. Our dissenting colleagues have raised several thoughtful rejoinders, but for the reasons I have outlined and others detailed in our opinion today, we conclude that the federal civilian employee called to active duty during a national emergency is entitled to differential pay while an emergency is ongoing without any further showing. Because the Federal Circuit held otherwise, its judgment is reversed and the case is remanded for further proceedings. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Alito, Kagan and Jackson joined.
